attributes.stem:
A 67-year-old man attends his General Practice clinic for a routine health check. He does not have any current health issues. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and drinks about two glasses of wine daily. He does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Abdominal examination reveals prominent dilated superficial veins across the anterior abdominal wall with cephalad flow (Image). The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying cause?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with dilated superficial abdominal wall veins, specifically the inferior epigastric veins, indicating obstruction of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with development of collateral circulation. The cephalad flow indicates blood is being redirected around an obstruction via collateral pathways.

Renal cell carcinoma commonly invades the renal vein and can extend into the IVC, causing obstruction. This patient's significant smoking history is a major risk factor. The absence of symptoms is common, as renal cell carcinoma is often detected incidentally before causing local symptoms.

Budd-Chiari syndrome, caused by hepatic vein thrombosis, typically presents with ascites, hepatomegaly, and abdominal pain. While it can cause IVC obstruction, it usually presents with more prominent symptoms and is less common in this age group.

Carcinoma of the lung rarely causes IVC obstruction unless there is direct invasion or significant mediastinal lymphadenopathy. In such cases, superior vena cava syndrome would be more likely, presenting with facial and upper body venous congestion.

Extension of iliac vein thrombosis would typically present with acute symptoms including leg swelling and pain. Chronic thrombosis could cause venous congestion but is less likely without previous history or risk factors for deep vein thrombosis.

Abdominal aortic aneurysm does not typically cause IVC obstruction unless it is very large. While this patient has risk factors for vascular disease, an aneurysm would not explain the pattern of venous obstruction seen here.
learningPoints: Dilated superficial abdominal wall veins suggest inferior vena cava obstruction requiring collateral circulation development.
Renal cell carcinoma commonly invades the renal vein and inferior vena cava, sometimes detected before other symptoms develop.
Smoking is a significant risk factor for renal cell carcinoma.
references: Joseph A, Scharbach S, Samant H. Anatomy, Anterolateral Abdominal Wall Veins. StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan. 2023 Aug 28. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK507909/

Almatari A, Sathe A, Wideman L, et al. Renal cell carcinoma with tumor thrombus: A review of relevant anatomy and surgical techniques for the general urologist. Urologic Oncology: Seminars and Original Investigations. 2023;41(4):153-165. Available at: 
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1078143922004859

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
